source.title: SafeDreamer: Safe Reinforcement Learning with World Models

confidence_rationale: SafeDreamer demonstrates directly that DreamerV3-style world model agents fail to achieve safety compliance without explicit constraint incorporation -- standard DreamerV3 'often fails to achieve zero-cost performance in complex scenarios.' The proposed fix (Lagrangian-based constraint planning within the world model) requires adding a structurally separate safety objective. This is the closest available evidence to the exact Q-012 question: a latent predictive world model cannot maintain agentic safety without explicit control constraints.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Standard DreamerV3 and comparable world-model RL agents fail to achieve zero-cost performance (safety compliance) in Safety-Gymnasium tasks; integrating Lagrangian constraint optimisation into the world model's planning phase is necessary and sufficient to achieve near-zero safety cost.
mapping.ree_translation: SafeDreamer provides an existence proof that the failure mode Q-012 identifies is real in practice: DreamerV3 without explicit safety constraints produces unsafe behaviour, and the remedy requires adding a structurally separate constraint pathway -- analogous to REE's explicit harm channel. The Lagrangian method is a mathematical proxy for what REE implements architecturally via z_harm and E3 harm evaluation.
mapping.mapping_caveat: SafeDreamer's 'safety' is defined by cost functions in Safety-Gymnasium benchmarks, which are engineering constraints (collision avoidance, velocity limits) rather than ethical harm in the REE sense. The paper does not test long-horizon goal-directed harm, value-laden tradeoffs, or situations where harm and reward are structurally coupled. The Lagrangian method also differs architecturally from REE's dedicated harm stream -- it is an optimisation-time penalty, not a separate learned forward model.
mapping.source_context: Safety-Gymnasium benchmark, DreamerV3-based agent, low-dimensional and visual observation tasks.

confidence_components.notes: ICLR 2024 paper using the exact architecture (DreamerV3) most relevant to Q-012. Mapping fidelity is good because the failure mode and fix are directly demonstrated. Transfer risk is moderate: Safety-Gymnasium 'safety' is narrower than REE 'harm', and the Lagrangian patch is architecturally different from a dedicated harm channel, so the strength of analogy should not be overstated.
